title: Why I remember smells better than faces
summary: Reflect on how a smell can bring back a clear memory, even when you have forgotten the people and places.
topic: A smell that brings back a strong memory
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences reflecting on a smell that brings back a clear memory, and how it is different from remembering faces or names. Describe the smell, the memory, and how it makes you feel. Use past simple, present simple, and the connectors 'when' and 'but'.
hintStarters: I cannot remember my old neighbour's face, but the moment I smell…
It is strange, because I forget names easily, yet…
The first time it happened, I was standing in…
I think smells stay with us because…
sampleText: I am bad with faces and worse with names. I meet someone three times and still forget who they are. But smells are different. When I smell wet earth after rain, I am eight years old again, standing in my grandmother's garden in Kerala. I cannot describe her face clearly anymore, and I do not remember the year she died, but I remember the smell of that garden as if I am standing in it now. The same thing happens with old soap, hospital floors, and the inside of certain buses. The memory comes back suddenly, without warning, and it is always stronger than the kind I get from photos. A photo shows me a face, but it stays outside me, like a picture on a wall. A smell goes straight in. I read once that the part of the brain for smell sits very close to the part for memory and feeling, so they share the same neighbourhood. I do not know if that is the real reason. What I know is that I can lose a whole year of my life from memory, but one smell can give a single afternoon back to me, complete and warm. It is a strange gift. I cannot choose when it comes, and I cannot ask for it. It just arrives, and then it fades again.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'When' for a trigger and 'but' for contrast
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'When I smell wet earth after rain, I am eight years old again' dùng 'when' để chỉ điều gì đó kích hoạt một phản ứng. Còn 'But smells are different' dùng 'but' để tạo sự tương phản với câu trước. Hai liên từ này giúp người viết so sánh và nối ý.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'When I smell wet earth after rain, I am eight years old again' uses 'when' to show something that triggers a reaction. Meanwhile, 'But smells are different' uses 'but' to contrast with the sentence before. These two connectors help the writer compare and link ideas.

sampleTranslationVi: Tôi rất kém nhớ mặt và càng kém nhớ tên. Tôi gặp ai đó ba lần mà vẫn quên họ là ai. Nhưng mùi thì khác. Khi tôi ngửi mùi đất ẩm sau cơn mưa, tôi lại tám tuổi, đứng trong khu vườn của bà ở Kerala. Tôi không còn tả rõ khuôn mặt bà nữa, và tôi không nhớ năm bà mất, nhưng tôi nhớ mùi khu vườn đó như thể tôi đang đứng trong đó lúc này. Điều tương tự xảy ra với mùi xà phòng cũ, sàn bệnh viện, và bên trong vài chiếc xe buýt. Ký ức trở lại đột ngột, không báo trước, và nó luôn mạnh hơn loại ký ức tôi có từ ảnh. Một bức ảnh cho tôi thấy một khuôn mặt, nhưng nó ở bên ngoài tôi, như một bức tranh trên tường. Một mùi đi thẳng vào trong. Tôi từng đọc rằng phần não phụ trách khứu giác nằm rất gần phần phụ trách ký ức và cảm xúc, nên chúng nằm chung một khu vực. Tôi không biết đó có phải lý do thật không. Điều tôi biết là tôi có thể đánh mất cả một năm của đời mình khỏi ký ức, nhưng một mùi có thể trả lại cho tôi một buổi chiều duy nhất, trọn vẹn và ấm áp. Đó là một món quà kỳ lạ. Tôi không thể chọn khi nào nó đến, và tôi không thể cầu xin nó. Nó cứ đến, rồi lại phai mờ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about a smell triggering a memory.
options: When I smell wet earth, I am eight years old again then.
When I smell wet earth, I am eight years old yet.
When I smell wet earth, I am eight years old again.
When I am smelling wet earth, I am eight years old again.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple 'smell' for a general trigger is correct. Option 2 adds an odd 'then'; 'yet' is the wrong word; the continuous 'am smelling' is unnatural for a general truth.
mcQuestions.hint: For a general truth or repeated trigger, present simple or continuous?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the most natural sentence contrasting smells and faces.
options: I forget faces easily, but smells stay by me.
I forget faces easy, but smells stay with me.
I forget faces easily, so smells stay with me.
I forget faces easily, but smells stay with me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'But' shows contrast and 'stay with me' is the right collocation. 'Stay by me' is unnatural; 'easy' should be 'easily'; 'so' shows result, not contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Do you need a contrast word or a result word here?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct comparative sentence about strength of memory.
options: The memory is stronger than the kind I get from photos.
The memory is more strong than the kind I get from photos.
The memory is stronger as the kind I get from photos.
The memory is the strongest than the kind I get from photos.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stronger than' is the correct comparative. 'More strong' is wrong; 'stronger as' should use 'than'; the superlative 'the strongest than' mixes forms.
mcQuestions.hint: Is the comparative of 'strong' formed with '-er' or 'more'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the most natural sentence about not describing a face.
options: I cannot to describe her face clearly anymore.
I cannot describe her face clearly anymore.
I cannot describe her face clear anymore.
I cannot describe clearly her face no more.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Cannot describe … clearly anymore' is correct. Option 2 adds 'to'; 'clear' should be the adverb 'clearly'; option 4 has awkward word order and a double negative feel.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'cannot', do we use 'describe' or 'to describe'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best sentence about the memory returning unexpectedly.
options: The memory comes back sudden, without warning.
The memory comes back suddenly, without any warn.
The memory comes back suddenly, without warning.
The memory is coming back suddenly, without warning always.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverb 'suddenly' and the phrase 'without warning' are correct. 'Sudden' is an adjective; 'warn' is a verb, not the noun 'warning'; the last adds an awkward 'always'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best sentence about a photo staying separate from you.
options: A photo shows to me a face, but it stays outside me.
A photo shows me a face, but it stays out of me.
A photo show me a face, but it stays outside me.
A photo shows me a face, but it stays outside me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Shows me a face' and 'stays outside me' are natural. 'Shows to me' adds an unneeded 'to'; 'out of me' changes the meaning; 'show' needs the third-person 's'.
mcQuestions.hint: Do we say 'shows me' or 'shows to me' something?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about the brain areas being close.
options: The part for smell sits close to the part for memory.
The part for smell sits close from the part for memory.
The part for smell sits closely to the part for memory.
The part for smell sit close to the part for memory.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Close to' is the correct collocation. 'Close from' is wrong; 'closely' is the wrong form here; 'sit' needs the third-person 's'.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition follows 'close' for distance: 'to' or 'from'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence about losing a year from memory.
options: I can lose a whole year of my life of memory.
I can lose a whole year of my life from memory.
I can losing a whole year of my life from memory.
I can lose a all year of my life from memory.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Lose … from memory' and 'a whole year' are correct. Option 2 uses 'of' instead of 'from'; 'can losing' is wrong after a modal; 'a all year' is ungrammatical.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about one smell returning an afternoon.
options: One smell can gives a single afternoon back to me.
One smell can give back a single afternoon at me.
One smell can give a single afternoon back to me.
One smell can give a single afternoon back for me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Give … back to me' is correct. Option 2 adds '-s' after 'can'; 'at me' and 'for me' are the wrong prepositions for giving back.
mcQuestions.hint: After the modal 'can', does the verb take an '-s'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best closing sentence about the memory fading.
options: It just arrives, and then it fade again.
It just arrive, and then it fades again.
It just arrives, and then it is fading again always.
It just arrives, and then it fades again.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Arrives' and 'fades' both take the third-person 's' correctly. Options 2 and 3 drop the 's'; option 4 misuses the continuous with 'always'.
mcQuestions.hint: With 'it' as the subject, what ending does the present-simple verb take?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do you think certain smells, sounds, or songs can bring back a moment more vividly than a photograph of it? What does that tell us about how memory really works?
